hypothesis: BASELINE_V1: Clean baseline with proven features — lags, rolling stats, price features, target encoding. Use Tweedie objective, num_leaves=64, learning_rate=0.05, n_estimators=800 with early stopping. No experimental features. Establish a clean baseline score.
Extend rolling windows to include 56-day window and add more lag features (lag_56

Apply WRMSSE-inspired sample weights during training: weight each training row by the item-store weight from the competition metric. Higher-revenue items get more weight, encouraging the model to focus on what matters for WRMSSE.
Add day-of-week × item target encoding (interaction encoding

Tune Tweedie variance power from 1.1 to 1.5 — retail sales follow a more heavily zero-inflated distribution, so a higher power may better fit the target distribution and reduce WRMSSE. All other settings unchanged from baseline.
Add 56-day rolling mean/std features and lag_56 to capture longer-term seasonal patterns (annual effects visible at ~8 weeks

Add richer price history features: rolling price mean/std over 7 and 28 days, price change from 7 days ago, and a price is at minimum in last 28 days binary flag. These capture promotional pricing patterns that drive spikes in sales for high-revenue items — which matter most for WRMSSE. All other settings unchanged from exp_0005.

Increase num_leaves from 128 to 256 to allow even more complex feature interactions. exp_0026 showed that increasing num_leaves from 64 to 128 improved WRMSSE from 0.97966 to 0.94711. Testing if further increasing to 256 continues this trend. All other settings unchanged from exp_0026.

Increase num_leaves from 256 to 512 to allow even more complex feature interactions. exp_0027 showed that increasing num_leaves from 128 to 256 improved WRMSSE from 0.94711 to 0.94675. Testing if further increasing to 512 continues this trend. To avoid overfitting with more leaves, also increase min_child_samples from 50 to 100 and increase lambda_l2 from 0.1 to 0.5 for stronger regularization. All other settings unchanged from exp_0027.

Add aggregated features: dept-level rolling mean, store-level rolling mean, and cat-level rolling mean as additional features for item-level prediction. These capture group-level demand signals that individual item lags may miss, especially for new/sparse items. Also add days-since-release (item release date

Add aggregated rolling mean features at dept, store, and cat level. These group-level demand signals complement individual item lags, especially for sparse items. dept_rmean_28, store_rmean_28, cat_rmean_28 capture the demand trend of the product group independently of the item. Also add days-since-release (item release date

Add price change flag (binary: did sell_price drop vs last week
Add price change flag feature: binary indicator of whether sell_price dropped vs last week (lag_7 of price
Add price change flag feature: binary indicator of whether sell_price dropped vs last week (lag_7 of price

Add price change flag feature: binary indicator of whether sell_price dropped vs last week. This is a simple, fast-to-compute feature that captures promotional pricing signals. price_drop_flag_7 = 1 if current price < price 7 days ago, else 0. Also add price_drop_flag_28 for monthly price drops. All other settings match best run (exp_0044, WRMSSE=0.94391

Add item release date feature: days_since_release = number of days since the first non-zero sale for each item. This captures item maturity and helps the model distinguish new items from established ones. New items often have different sales patterns that confuse lag-based models. All other settings match best run (exp_0044, WRMSSE=0.94391

Add zero sale streak feature: count of consecutive days with zero sales immediately before each observation. This captures whether items are temporarily out of stock or discontinued, which is a strong signal for sparse items. zero_streak = 0 means the item was selling; higher values indicate prolonged absence. All other settings match best run (exp_0044, WRMSSE=0.94391
Add days-to-next-event and days-from-last-event features. Distance to nearest calendar event captures pre-event stockpiling and post-event demand drops. These are fast to compute (no groupby rolling
Add price drop flag features (binary: did sell_price drop vs 7 days ago, and vs 28 days ago
Add price drop flag features using fast vectorized computation (no groupby rolling
Add days_since_release feature using fast vectorized computation (no groupby rolling

Add days_since_release feature using fast vectorized computation. days_since_release = number of days since first non-zero sale for each item. Uses cumsum trick to avoid slow groupby rolling. Captures item maturity and helps model distinguish new vs established items. All other settings match best run (exp_0044, WRMSSE=0.94391
Apply WRMSSE-based sample weighting during training. High-revenue items contribute more to the WRMSSE metric. By passing item-level weights (derived from the weights array in build_dataset

Add days_since_release feature using fast vectorized computation. days_since_release = number of days since first non-zero sale for each item. Uses pivot + cumsum trick to avoid slow groupby rolling. Captures item maturity and helps model distinguish new vs established items. Previous attempt (exp_0061
Add days_since_release feature: number of days since first non-zero sale for each item. Uses vectorized pivot approach from exp_0063 which succeeded (WRMSSE=0.95290
Add days_since_release feature (fast vectorized approach that succeeded in exp_0063
Add days_since_release feature using fast vectorized computation (exp_0063 approach

Add price drop flag features: binary indicator for whether sell_price dropped vs 7 days ago and vs 28 days ago. Price drops are strong purchase drivers. Uses fast vectorized shift operations (no groupby rolling

Add price drop flag features: binary indicator for whether sell_price dropped vs 7 days ago and vs 28 days ago. Price drops are strong purchase drivers. Uses fast vectorized shift operations (no groupby rolling

Add zero_sale_streak feature: number of consecutive days of zero sales before the current day. This captures item availability/stockout patterns. A long streak of zeros suggests item may be unavailable, and the model should predict near-zero. Computed using vectorized cumsum trick (no loops

Tune Tweedie variance power to 1.2 and num_leaves to 255 with learning_rate=0.03. Previous Tweedie 1.2 tests held num_leaves at 256 — trying 255 with reduced lr for potentially better generalization. Based on next priority suggestions.

Tune num_leaves to 512 with learning_rate=0.03 and Tweedie variance power=1.2. Previous experiments with num_leaves=255/256 and Tweedie 1.2 at lr=0.03/0.04 all scored 0.94513. Trying num_leaves=512 to give the model more capacity to capture complex interactions, combined with Tweedie 1.2 and lr=0.03. Deeper trees with more leaves may better capture the feature interactions from our rich feature set (lag_365, dow_roll_mean, etc

Try Tweedie variance power=1.2 with num_leaves=256 and learning_rate=0.025. Previous experiments with Tweedie 1.2 at lr=0.03/0.04 and num_leaves=255/256/512 all scored around 0.94513. Reducing learning_rate further to 0.025 with more estimators may allow better convergence and generalization. This is a careful sweep of the lr dimension which hasnt been tried at 0.025.

Add zero_sale_streak feature: count of consecutive days with zero sales ending on the previous day. Computed via vectorized groupby+cumsum approach. This captures demand intermittency — items with long zero streaks are less likely to sell. Combined with current best settings (min_child_samples=100, lambda_l2=1.0, lambda_l1=0.1, num_leaves=256, lr=0.04, tweedie=1.5
